note: Delaware sheriffs have limited civil-process powers only (serve court process, conduct sheriff's/foreclosure sales); no general arrest or police powers after 2012 statute (HB 325) and the 2013 Delaware Supreme Court ruling in Christopher v. Sussex County. Partisan-elected, 4-yr term; Delaware sheriff elections fall in 2022/2026/2030 (per DE Dept of Elections schedule), so last elected Nov 2022, term through Dec 31, 2026, next election Nov 2026 — not 2024.

Delaware sheriffs have limited civil-process powers only (serve court process, conduct sheriff's/foreclosure sales); no general arrest or police powers after 2012 statute (HB 325) and the 2013 Delaware Supreme Court ruling in Christopher v. Sussex County. Partisan-elected, 4-yr term; Delaware sheriff elections fall in 2022/2026/2030, so last elected Nov 2022, term through Dec 31, 2026, next election Nov 2026 — not 2024.

Delaware sheriffs have limited civil-process powers only (serve court process, conduct sheriff's/foreclosure sales); no general arrest or police powers after 2012 statute (HB 325) and the 2013 Delaware Supreme Court ruling in Christopher v. Sussex County (Sussex was the county in that dispute). Partisan-elected, 4-yr term; Delaware sheriff elections fall in 2022/2026/2030, so last elected Nov 2022, term through Dec 31, 2026, next election Nov 2026 — not 2024.

Delaware has 3 counties (New Castle, Kent, Sussex), each with a partisan-elected sheriff (4-yr term). Unlike the standard model, Delaware sheriffs have LIMITED powers: civil process and court/judicial service and conducting sheriff's (foreclosure/tax) sales only. They do NOT have general law-enforcement arrest or police powers — 2012 legislation (HB 325) declared sheriffs and deputies are not police officers with arrest authority, upheld by the Delaware Supreme Court in 2013 (Christopher v. Sussex County) after a Sussex County dispute. Current officeholders: New Castle — N. Scott Phillips (D); Kent — Brian E. Lewis (D); Sussex — Robert T. Lee (R). Delaware sheriff elections are held in 2022/2026/2030 (DE Dept of Elections schedule); the most recent was Nov 2022 and the next is Nov 2026 (not 2024).

Connecticut has NO county sheriffs. The elected county-sheriff system was ABOLISHED by a constitutional amendment approved by voters on Nov 7, 2000 (repealing Art. IV §25 of the state constitution), effective Nov 30, 2000, after corruption scandals. Connecticut's counties have no county government. Former sheriff functions were transferred: courthouse security and prisoner transport/custody went to the Judicial Branch as judicial marshals; service of civil process went to state marshals, overseen by the autonomous State Marshal Commission in the Judicial Department. No elected county sheriffs exist.

Alaska has NO counties and NO county sheriffs. The state is organized into boroughs plus a single Unorganized Borough (with no borough-wide government), and neither boroughs nor the unorganized borough elect sheriffs. Statewide/rural law enforcement is provided by the Alaska State Troopers (Dept of Public Safety), who also serve civil process; local police departments cover incorporated cities; and Village Public Safety Officers (VPSOs) provide policing in remote Native villages. No elected county sheriffs exist.

Hawaii has NO elected county sheriffs. Sheriff functions are performed by a STATEWIDE Sheriff Division, which since Jan 1, 2024 sits under the new Hawaii Department of Law Enforcement (DLE) — split off from the former Dept of Public Safety under Act 278 / HB 2171. The DLE's sheriff deputies provide security and law enforcement for state facilities, the airport, and courts. Hawaii's 4 counties (Honolulu, Hawaii, Maui, Kauai) each run their own county police departments led by an appointed chief; there is no county sheriff. Current DLE Director: Mike Lambert (nominated Jan 2025 by Gov. Green, confirmed 2025).

Rhode Island has NO elected county sheriffs. Sheriffs are STATE employees, APPOINTED (not elected), organized nominally by the 5 counties but centrally administered as the Division of Sheriffs within the RI Department of Public Safety (R.I. Gen. Laws §42-7.3-3.2). The division handles courthouse security, prisoner transport, and service of civil process statewide. It is led by a Chief Sheriff: Paul M. Martellini, sworn in Oct 16, 2024 (acting since April 2024). No elected county sheriffs exist.
